The writer of Hebrews begins by striking a note of urgency that should unsettle you. We must pay closer attention—not because the message is merely interesting or spiritually enriching, but because drifting is devastatingly easy. The comparison at hand is stark: if the law given through angels carried binding force and real consequences, how much more serious is neglecting the salvation announced by the Lord Himself and confirmed through miracles and the Holy Spirit's power? This is not a call to greater effort or striving. It is a warning that there is something to lose, and losing it is possible. The word "escape" in verse 3 carries the weight of judgment. You cannot neglect a great salvation without consequence.

What makes this salvation so great becomes clear in what follows: it concerns Jesus Christ, who was made a little lower than the angels for a specific purpose—to suffer death for everyone. This is the staggering hinge upon which everything turns. The psalmist's words about humanity being crowned with glory and having all things placed under their feet find their true fulfillment not in Adam or in us, but in Christ alone, the one who actually accomplished what humanity was created to do. Yet Christ accomplished it through an almost unimaginable path: voluntary humiliation, real suffering, real death. He did this so that by His grace He might taste death for everyone—bearing the death penalty that belonged to you and to all who believe. This is the salvation you must not drift away from. This is the deliverance purchased at immeasurable cost.

Notice the stunning intimacy at work here. Jesus is not ashamed to call you His brother or sister. He shared fully in your humanity—flesh and blood, real temptation, genuine suffering—not as some distant transaction but as someone who understands your struggle from the inside. He became a merciful and faithful high priest precisely because He experienced what you experience. His suffering was not symbolic; it was the necessary path to making atonement for sins. And in doing this, He destroyed the devil's power and liberated all who were enslaved by fear of death throughout their lives. Death no longer has dominion over those who belong to Christ.

Yet the warning remains. The great salvation is real. Its cost was infinite. Its offer is genuine. But so is the possibility of drifting—of treating it as merely background noise, of being comforted by its truth without being changed by it. The question Hebrews poses to you is not theoretical. How will you respond to this salvation? Will you draw near to the One who shed His blood for you, or will the current of indifference slowly carry you away?
